summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Earth's Layers (Crust, Mantle, Core)
summary_sections.content_html:
Earth has four main layers, each with distinct properties:

  • Crust: the thin, rocky outer layer (5–70 km thick). Oceanic crust is denser (basalt); continental crust is thicker and less dense (granite).
  • Mantle: the thickest layer (~2,900 km). The upper mantle contains the partially molten asthenosphere, which allows tectonic plates to move.
  • Outer Core: liquid iron and nickel (~2,200 km thick). Convection here generates Earth's magnetic field.
  • Inner Core: solid iron and nickel (~1,200 km radius), kept solid by immense pressure despite extreme temperatures (~5,400 °C).

Scientists study Earth's interior using seismic waves—P-waves travel through solids and liquids; S-waves travel only through solids.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Mineral Identification
summary_sections.content_html:
Minerals are naturally occurring, inorganic solids with a definite chemical composition and crystalline structure.

  • Hardness: resistance to scratching, measured by Mohs Hardness Scale (1 = talc, 10 = diamond)
  • Luster: how a mineral reflects light (metallic vs. non-metallic: glassy, waxy, pearly, earthy)
  • Streak: the color of a mineral's powder when rubbed on an unglazed porcelain plate; more reliable than surface color
  • Cleavage: tendency to break along flat planes of weakness
  • Fracture: irregular breaking pattern (conchoidal, fibrous, etc.)
  • Specific gravity: ratio of a mineral's density to water's density

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Types of Rocks (Igneous, Sedimentary, Metamorphic)
summary_sections.content_html:
All rocks belong to one of three major categories based on how they form:

  • Igneous rocks form from the cooling and solidification of magma or lava.
    • Intrusive (plutonic): cool slowly underground → large crystals (e.g., granite)
    • Extrusive (volcanic): cool quickly at the surface → small/no crystals (e.g., basalt, obsidian)
  • Sedimentary rocks form from compaction and cementation of sediments, or from chemical/biological processes.
    • Clastic: made of rock fragments (sandstone, shale)
    • Chemical: precipitated from solution (limestone, rock salt)
    • Organic: from remains of organisms (coal, some limestone)
  • Metamorphic rocks form when existing rocks are transformed by heat and/or pressure without melting.
    • Foliated: layered appearance (slate, gneiss)
    • Non-foliated: no layers (marble, quartzite)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: The Rock Cycle
summary_sections.content_html:
The rock cycle describes how rocks continuously transform from one type to another through geological processes:

  • Igneous → Sedimentary: weathering and erosion break igneous rock into sediments, which are deposited, compacted, and cemented.
  • Sedimentary → Metamorphic: burial increases heat and pressure, transforming sedimentary rock.
  • Metamorphic → Igneous: further heat causes melting to magma, which cools into igneous rock.
  • Any rock type can become any other type—the cycle has no fixed starting point.
  • Uplift exposes buried rocks to weathering; subduction pulls surface rocks deep into the mantle.

The rock cycle is driven by Earth's internal heat (plate tectonics, volcanism) and external energy from the Sun (weathering, erosion).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Soil Formation & Composition
summary_sections.content_html:
Soil is a mixture of weathered rock (minerals), organic matter (humus), water, and air.

  • Soil formation (pedogenesis) depends on five factors: parent material, climate, topography, biological activity, and time.
  • Soil horizons (layers from surface to bedrock):
    • O horizon: organic matter (leaf litter, humus)
    • A horizon (topsoil): rich in organic matter and minerals; most fertile
    • B horizon (subsoil): accumulates minerals leached from above (clay, iron oxides)
    • C horizon: partially weathered parent material
    • R horizon: unweathered bedrock
  • Soil texture depends on proportions of sand (largest), silt, and clay (smallest) particles. Loam—a balanced mix—is ideal for agriculture.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Continental Drift & Evidence
summary_sections.content_html:
Continental drift is the theory proposed by Alfred Wegener in 1912 that continents were once joined in a supercontinent called Pangaea and have since drifted apart.

  • Evidence:
    • Jigsaw fit of coastlines (especially South America and Africa)
    • Matching fossil distributions across now-separated continents (e.g., Glossopteris, Mesosaurus)
    • Similar rock sequences and mountain chains on different continents
    • Paleoclimate evidence: glacial deposits in tropical regions, coal in polar regions
  • Wegener's theory was initially rejected because he couldn't explain the mechanism for how continents moved.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Seafloor Spreading
summary_sections.content_html:
Seafloor spreading is the process by which new oceanic crust forms at mid-ocean ridges and moves outward.

  • Proposed by Harry Hess in 1962, it provided the mechanism Wegener's theory lacked.
  • At mid-ocean ridges, magma rises from the mantle, solidifies, and pushes older crust outward on both sides.
  • Evidence:
    • Magnetic striping: alternating patterns of normal and reversed magnetic polarity in ocean floor rocks, symmetric about ridges
    • Age of oceanic crust increases with distance from the ridge
    • Sediment thickness increases with distance from the ridge
  • Seafloor spreading explains why oceanic crust is young (< 200 million years) compared to continental crust (up to 4 billion years).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Plate Boundaries (Convergent, Divergent, Transform)
summary_sections.content_html:
Earth's lithosphere is divided into tectonic plates that interact at three types of plate boundaries:

  • Divergent: plates move apart. Magma rises to fill the gap, creating new crust. Examples: Mid-Atlantic Ridge, East African Rift.
  • Convergent: plates collide.
    • Oceanic–oceanic: denser plate subducts → deep-ocean trenches, volcanic island arcs
    • Oceanic–continental: oceanic plate subducts → mountain ranges, volcanoes (Andes)
    • Continental–continental: neither subducts → folded mountain ranges (Himalayas)
  • Transform: plates slide horizontally past each other. Produces earthquakes but no volcanism. Example: San Andreas Fault.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Earthquakes & Seismic Waves
summary_sections.content_html:
An earthquake is a sudden release of energy in Earth's lithosphere caused by movement along faults.

  • Focus (hypocenter): the point underground where the earthquake originates
  • Epicenter: the point on the surface directly above the focus
  • Seismic waves:
    • P-waves (primary): fastest, compressional, travel through solids and liquids
    • S-waves (secondary): slower, shear motion, travel through solids only
    • Surface waves: slowest but most destructive, travel along Earth's surface
  • Richter scale (magnitude): measures energy released; each whole number = 10× amplitude, ~32× energy
  • Most earthquakes occur along plate boundaries, especially the Ring of Fire around the Pacific.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Volcanoes & Hot Spots
summary_sections.content_html:
Volcanoes form where magma reaches Earth's surface. They are associated with convergent boundaries, divergent boundaries, and hot spots.

  • Types of volcanoes:
    • Shield volcanoes: broad, gently sloping; built by low-viscosity basaltic lava flows (e.g., Mauna Loa)
    • Cinder cone: small, steep; built from ejected lava fragments
    • Composite (stratovolcano): tall, steep; alternating layers of lava and ash; explosive eruptions (e.g., Mount St. Helens)
  • Hot spots: volcanic areas over unusually hot zones in the mantle, not at plate boundaries. Plates move over the stationary hot spot, creating chains of volcanoes (e.g., Hawaiian Islands).
  • Volcanic eruptions release gases (CO₂, SO₂, H₂O), ash, and lava—they can affect global climate.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Weathering (Mechanical & Chemical)
summary_sections.content_html:
Weathering is the breakdown of rocks at or near Earth's surface. It does not involve transport (that's erosion).

  • Mechanical (physical) weathering: rocks break into smaller pieces without changing composition.
    • Frost wedging: water freezes in cracks, expanding and splitting rock
    • Root pry: plant roots grow into cracks
    • Abrasion: rock-on-rock grinding by wind, water, or ice
    • Thermal expansion: repeated heating and cooling causes stress fractures
  • Chemical weathering: minerals in rock are chemically altered or dissolved.
    • Oxidation: iron-bearing minerals react with oxygen (rusting)
    • Hydrolysis: minerals react with water to form clay
    • Carbonation: CO₂ + water forms carbonic acid, dissolving limestone (karst landscapes)
    • Acid rain accelerates chemical weathering
  • Chemical weathering is fastest in warm, wet climates.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Erosion & Deposition
summary_sections.content_html:
Erosion is the transport of weathered material by natural agents; deposition is the settling of that material in a new location.

  • Agents of erosion:
    • Water: rivers, streams, runoff—the most powerful agent of erosion worldwide
    • Wind: moves fine particles (sand, silt); dominant in deserts
    • Ice: glaciers carve valleys and transport massive boulders
    • Gravity: mass movements (landslides, mudflows, creep)
  • Deposition occurs when the transporting agent loses energy:
    • Rivers slow → deposit sediment in floodplains, deltas
    • Wind slows → sand dunes, loess deposits
    • Glaciers melt → moraines, glacial till
  • The largest particles are deposited first; the finest (clay) travel farthest.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: River Systems & Landforms
summary_sections.content_html:
Rivers shape landscapes through erosion, transport, and deposition, creating distinctive landforms:

  • Upper course: steep gradient, fast flow, V-shaped valleys, waterfalls, rapids
  • Middle course: moderate gradient, wider valley, meanders begin to form
  • Lower course: gentle gradient, wide floodplain, meandering river, oxbow lakes, deltas
  • Key features:
    • Meanders: S-shaped curves; erosion on the outer bank, deposition on the inner bank
    • Oxbow lakes: formed when a meander loop is cut off from the main river
    • Floodplain: flat area adjacent to a river that floods periodically, receiving fertile sediment
    • Drainage basin (watershed): the total area drained by a river and its tributaries

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Glaciers & Ice Ages
summary_sections.content_html:
Glaciers are large, persistent bodies of ice that form from compacted snow and move under their own weight.

  • Types: alpine (valley) glaciers and continental ice sheets
  • How glaciers shape the land:
    • Erosion: plucking (pulling rock from bedrock) and abrasion (grinding with embedded rock)
    • Creates U-shaped valleys, cirques, arêtes, horns, fjords
  • Deposition: moraines, drumlins, glacial till, erratics, outwash plains
  • Ice ages: periods when glaciers covered much of Earth's surface. The most recent ended ~11,700 years ago.
  • Milankovitch cycles: variations in Earth's orbit (eccentricity, axial tilt, precession) that trigger glacial/interglacial periods.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Mass Wasting & Landslides
summary_sections.content_html:
Mass wasting (mass movement) is the downslope movement of rock, soil, and debris under the influence of gravity.

  • Types (by speed and material):
    • Rockfall: sudden free-fall of rock from a cliff
    • Landslide: rapid sliding of a mass of rock/soil along a slope
    • Mudflow/debris flow: rapid flow of water-saturated material
    • Creep: very slow, gradual downslope movement (tilts fences, trees, headstones)
  • Factors that increase risk: steep slopes, heavy rainfall (saturates soil), removal of vegetation, earthquakes, undercutting of slopes by erosion or construction
  • Angle of repose: the steepest angle at which loose material remains stable without sliding

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Relative Dating (Superposition, Cross-Cutting)
summary_sections.content_html:
Relative dating determines the order of geological events without assigning specific ages.

  • Law of Superposition: in undisturbed sedimentary layers, the oldest rocks are at the bottom, youngest at the top.
  • Principle of Original Horizontality: sedimentary layers are originally deposited horizontally; tilted layers indicate later deformation.
  • Principle of Cross-Cutting Relationships: a fault or intrusion is younger than the rock it cuts through.
  • Principle of Inclusion: fragments of rock included within another rock are older than the surrounding rock.
  • Unconformities: gaps in the rock record showing missing time (angular unconformity, disconformity, nonconformity).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Absolute Dating & Radiometric Methods
summary_sections.content_html:
Absolute (numerical) dating assigns specific ages in years using radioactive decay.

  • Radioactive decay: unstable parent isotopes decay into stable daughter isotopes at a constant rate.
  • Half-life: the time for half of a radioactive sample to decay. Each isotope has a unique half-life.
    • Carbon-14: ~5,730 years (organic material up to ~50,000 years)
    • Potassium-40 → Argon-40: 1.25 billion years (igneous rocks)
    • Uranium-238 → Lead-206: 4.5 billion years (oldest rocks)
  • Scientists measure the parent-to-daughter ratio to calculate age.
  • After 1 half-life: 50% parent remains; after 2: 25%; after 3: 12.5%, etc.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: The Geologic Time Scale
summary_sections.content_html:
The geologic time scale divides Earth's 4.6-billion-year history into hierarchical units:

  • Eons → Eras → Periods → Epochs
  • Major divisions:
    • Precambrian (4.6 Ga–541 Ma): ~88% of Earth's history; simple life (bacteria, algae); formation of atmosphere and oceans
    • Paleozoic Era (541–252 Ma): 'ancient life'; Cambrian explosion, first fish, amphibians, reptiles, forests; ends with largest mass extinction
    • Mesozoic Era (252–66 Ma): 'age of reptiles/dinosaurs'; first mammals, birds, flowering plants; ends with asteroid impact extinction
    • Cenozoic Era (66 Ma–present): 'age of mammals'; rise of primates, grasslands, ice ages
  • Boundaries between eras are defined by mass extinction events.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Fossils & the Fossil Record
summary_sections.content_html:
Fossils are preserved remains, traces, or impressions of organisms from past geological ages.

  • Types of fossils:
    • Body fossils: actual preserved remains (bones, shells, teeth, whole organisms in amber or ice)
    • Trace fossils: evidence of activity (footprints, burrows, coprolites)
    • Molds and casts: impressions or filled-in impressions of organisms in rock
    • Petrification: minerals replace original organic material (petrified wood)
  • Index fossils: fossils of organisms that lived during a short, well-defined time period over a wide geographic area—used to date rock layers.
  • Conditions for fossilization: rapid burial, hard body parts, low-oxygen environments (prevent decomposition).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Mass Extinctions & Evolution
summary_sections.content_html:
Mass extinctions are catastrophic events where a significant percentage of all species go extinct in a geologically short time.

  • Big Five mass extinctions:
    1. End-Ordovician (~444 Ma): 85% of species lost; glaciation
    2. Late Devonian (~372 Ma): 75%; ocean anoxia
    3. End-Permian (~252 Ma): ~96% of species; 'The Great Dying'; volcanic activity
    4. End-Triassic (~201 Ma): ~80%; volcanic CO₂ and warming
    5. End-Cretaceous (~66 Ma): ~76%; asteroid impact + volcanism
  • After each mass extinction, surviving species diversify to fill empty niches (adaptive radiation).
  • Example: mammals diversified rapidly after the end-Cretaceous extinction that eliminated non-avian dinosaurs.
  • Some scientists argue we are in a sixth mass extinction caused by human activities.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Composition & Layers of the Atmosphere
summary_sections.content_html:
Earth's atmosphere is a thin layer of gases essential for life:

  • Composition: 78% N₂, 21% O₂, ~1% Ar, ~0.04% CO₂, plus water vapor and trace gases
  • Layers (from surface upward):
    • Troposphere (0–12 km): weather occurs here; temperature decreases with altitude
    • Stratosphere (12–50 km): contains the ozone layer; temperature increases
    • Mesosphere (50–80 km): coldest layer; meteors burn up here
    • Thermosphere (80–700 km): very hot but thin; auroras occur here; includes the ionosphere
    • Exosphere (>700 km): outermost layer; transitions into space

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Energy Transfer (Radiation, Conduction, Convection)
summary_sections.content_html:
Energy from the Sun drives Earth's weather and climate through three heat transfer mechanisms:

  • Radiation: transfer of energy through electromagnetic waves; how solar energy reaches Earth through the vacuum of space
  • Conduction: transfer of heat through direct contact between molecules; heats the air directly touching warm surfaces
  • Convection: transfer of heat through the movement of fluids (liquids and gases); warm air rises, cool air sinks, creating convection cells that drive wind and weather

Earth's energy budget: ~30% of incoming solar radiation is reflected (albedo), ~70% is absorbed by the surface and atmosphere. Earth re-radiates energy as infrared radiation, some of which is trapped by greenhouse gases.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Air Pressure, Wind & Coriolis Effect
summary_sections.content_html:
Air pressure is the weight of the atmosphere pressing down on a surface. It decreases with altitude.

  • Wind blows from high pressure to low pressure areas.
  • The pressure gradient force drives wind speed—the steeper the gradient, the faster the wind.
  • The Coriolis effect: Earth's rotation deflects moving air to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern Hemisphere.
  • Global wind patterns:
    • Trade winds: 0°–30° latitude (easterlies)
    • Westerlies: 30°–60° latitude
    • Polar easterlies: 60°–90° latitude
  • Jet streams: narrow bands of fast-moving air in the upper troposphere that influence weather patterns.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Cloud Types & Precipitation
summary_sections.content_html:
Clouds form when air rises, cools to its dew point, and water vapor condenses on tiny particles (condensation nuclei).

  • Cloud types by altitude:
    • High clouds (>6 km): cirrus, cirrostratus, cirrocumulus — thin, wispy, made of ice crystals
    • Middle clouds (2–6 km): altostratus, altocumulus — gray or white sheets or layers
    • Low clouds (<2 km): stratus, stratocumulus, nimbostratus — thick, gray, may produce steady precipitation
    • Vertical development: cumulus (fair weather) → cumulonimbus (thunderstorms; extend through all levels)
  • Precipitation forms: rain, snow, sleet, freezing rain, hail
  • Precipitation occurs when cloud droplets grow large enough to fall — through collision-coalescence (warm clouds) or the Bergeron process (cold clouds with ice + supercooled water).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Weather Fronts & Storms
summary_sections.content_html:
A front is the boundary between two air masses of different temperature and humidity.

  • Cold front: cold air pushes under warm air, forcing it up rapidly → towering clouds, heavy rain/thunderstorms, then clearing and cooler temperatures
  • Warm front: warm air slides over retreating cold air → gradual cloud buildup, steady light precipitation
  • Stationary front: neither air mass advances → prolonged cloudiness and precipitation
  • Occluded front: a cold front overtakes a warm front → complex weather, precipitation

Storms:

  • Thunderstorms: caused by strong convection; produce lightning, heavy rain, wind, sometimes hail and tornadoes
  • Hurricanes (tropical cyclones): form over warm ocean water (≥26.5°C); categories 1–5 on Saffir-Simpson scale
  • Tornadoes: violently rotating columns of air; measured on Enhanced Fujita (EF) scale

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Climate vs. Weather
summary_sections.content_html:
Weather is the short-term state of the atmosphere at a specific place and time (temperature, precipitation, wind, humidity).

Climate is the average of weather conditions over a long period (usually 30+ years) for a region.

  • Weather changes day to day; climate represents long-term patterns and expectations.
  • Climate is described by average temperature, precipitation, seasonal patterns, and extreme events.
  • 'Climate is what you expect; weather is what you get.'

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Factors Affecting Climate
summary_sections.content_html:
Several factors determine the climate of a region:

  • Latitude: the most important factor; higher latitudes receive less direct solar energy → cooler climates
  • Altitude: temperature decreases ~6.5°C per 1,000 m elevation gain
  • Ocean currents: warm currents (Gulf Stream) moderate coastal climates; cold currents cool and dry adjacent coasts
  • Distance from the ocean (continentality): oceans moderate temperature; interiors have more extreme seasonal swings
  • Topography: mountain ranges create rain shadow effects—moist air rises on the windward side (rain), descends as dry air on the leeward side (desert)
  • Prevailing winds: determine moisture transport and precipitation patterns

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Climate Zones & Biomes
summary_sections.content_html:
Earth's climates are classified into zones that correspond to distinct biomes (large ecological regions):

  • Tropical (0°–23.5°): warm year-round, high rainfall → tropical rainforests, savannas
  • Dry/Arid: low precipitation → deserts, steppes
  • Temperate (~23.5°–66.5°): moderate temperatures, distinct seasons → deciduous forests, Mediterranean
  • Continental (interior, mid-latitudes): large seasonal temperature swings → prairies, boreal forests
  • Polar (>66.5°): very cold, low precipitation → tundra, ice caps

The Köppen climate classification system uses temperature and precipitation data to define climate types (A=tropical, B=arid, C=temperate, D=continental, E=polar).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: The Greenhouse Effect
summary_sections.content_html:
The greenhouse effect is a natural process that warms Earth's surface:

  • Sunlight (short-wave radiation) passes through the atmosphere and warms Earth's surface.
  • Earth re-emits energy as infrared (long-wave) radiation.
  • Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O vapor, O₃) absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, trapping heat in the atmosphere.
  • Without the greenhouse effect, Earth's average temperature would be about -18°C instead of +15°C.
  • The enhanced greenhouse effect: human activities (burning fossil fuels, deforestation, agriculture) increase greenhouse gas concentrations, trapping more heat and causing global warming.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Evidence of Climate Change
summary_sections.content_html:
Multiple independent lines of evidence confirm that global climate is changing:

  • Temperature records: global average has risen ~1.1°C since the late 1800s; 20 of the 21 warmest years on record have occurred since 2000
  • Ice cores: air bubbles trapped in ancient ice reveal past CO₂ levels and temperatures over 800,000 years
  • Sea-level rise: ~20 cm since 1900 due to thermal expansion and melting ice
  • Glacial retreat: glaciers worldwide are shrinking
  • Arctic sea ice decline: summer minimum extent has decreased ~40% since 1979
  • Ocean acidification: CO₂ dissolving in seawater lowers pH
  • Ecosystem changes: shifting species ranges, earlier spring events, coral bleaching

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Human Impact on Climate
summary_sections.content_html:
Human activities are the primary driver of current climate change:

  • Burning fossil fuels: coal, oil, natural gas combustion releases CO₂—the largest source of greenhouse gas emissions (~75%)
  • Deforestation: removes carbon sinks (trees absorb CO₂); burning/decomposing trees release stored carbon
  • Agriculture: livestock produce CH₄; rice paddies release CH₄; fertilizers release N₂O
  • Industry: cement production, manufacturing processes release CO₂ and other gases

Mitigation strategies: renewable energy, energy efficiency, reforestation, carbon capture, international agreements (Paris Agreement), reducing consumption

Adaptation strategies: building sea walls, developing drought-resistant crops, improving warning systems, urban planning

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Ocean Floor Features
summary_sections.content_html:
The ocean floor has distinct topographic features shaped by plate tectonics:

  • Continental shelf: shallow, gently sloping extension of the continent underwater (avg. depth ~130 m)
  • Continental slope: steep drop-off from the shelf to the deep ocean
  • Abyssal plain: vast, flat, deep ocean floor (3,000–6,000 m depth) covered in fine sediment
  • Mid-ocean ridge: underwater mountain chain formed at divergent plate boundaries (longest mountain range on Earth)
  • Ocean trench: deep, narrow depression at convergent boundaries (e.g., Mariana Trench ~11,000 m deep)
  • Seamounts: underwater volcanic mountains; flat-topped ones called guyots

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Ocean Water Properties
summary_sections.content_html:
Seawater's physical and chemical properties affect all marine processes:

  • Salinity: average ~35 parts per thousand (ppt); mainly NaCl. Varies with evaporation, precipitation, river input, and ice formation.
  • Temperature: ranges from -2°C (polar) to 30°C (tropical surface). Decreases with depth; the thermocline is the zone of rapid temperature change.
  • Density: depends on temperature (cold = denser) and salinity (saltier = denser). Drives deep ocean circulation.
  • Dissolved gases: O₂ (essential for marine life) and CO₂ (affects pH). Colder water holds more dissolved gas.
  • pH: normally ~8.1 (slightly alkaline); decreasing due to ocean acidification from absorbed CO₂.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Ocean Currents & Circulation
summary_sections.content_html:
Ocean currents are large-scale movements of seawater driven by wind, density differences, and the Coriolis effect:

  • Surface currents (upper ~400 m): driven by prevailing winds. Form circular patterns called gyres—clockwise in Northern Hemisphere, counterclockwise in Southern.
  • Deep currents (thermohaline circulation): driven by density differences from temperature and salinity. Cold, salty water sinks at polar regions and flows along the ocean floor toward the equator.
  • Upwelling: cold, nutrient-rich deep water rises to the surface (often near coasts). Supports highly productive fisheries.
  • The global conveyor belt: a connected system of surface and deep currents that circulates water, heat, and nutrients around the globe.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Tides & Their Causes
summary_sections.content_html:
Tides are the regular rise and fall of ocean water levels caused by the gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun:

  • The Moon has the greatest tidal influence (closer to Earth than the Sun).
  • Tides produce two bulges: one facing the Moon (gravitational pull) and one on the opposite side (centrifugal effect).
  • Spring tides: Sun and Moon aligned (new moon, full moon) → largest tidal range (highest high tides, lowest low tides)
  • Neap tides: Sun and Moon at right angles (first/third quarter) → smallest tidal range
  • Most coasts experience two high tides and two low tides per day (semidiurnal), though some have diurnal (one cycle) or mixed patterns.
  • Tidal range varies with coastal geography—the Bay of Fundy has the world's largest tides (~16 m).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Marine Ecosystems
summary_sections.content_html:
Earth's oceans contain diverse ecosystems, each with unique conditions and organisms:

  • Coral reefs: warm, shallow tropical waters; built by coral polyps with symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae); high biodiversity ('rainforests of the sea')
  • Estuaries: where rivers meet the ocean; brackish water; highly productive nursery habitats
  • Kelp forests: temperate coastal waters; giant kelp provides habitat for hundreds of species
  • Open ocean (pelagic zone): vast but relatively low productivity; phytoplankton are the primary producers
  • Deep-sea ecosystems: dark, cold, high pressure; organisms rely on marine snow or chemosynthesis at hydrothermal vents
  • Intertidal zone: coastline exposed between tides; organisms must tolerate air exposure, wave action, and salinity changes
